title: Charged Bose polarons at finite momentum
authors: Grover Andrade Sánchez, Arturo Camacho Guardian
abstract: Charged impurities in quantum fluids have unveiled new classes of strongly correlated many-body states across condensed matter, ultracold gases, and hybrid atom-ion platforms. While previous studies have primarily focused on their ground-state and static properties, much less is known about their finite-momentum behavior, which governs transport, dissipation, and quasiparticle stability. Here, we investigate the momentum-dependent properties of a charged Bose polaron using a diagrammatic approach within second-order perturbation theory, explicitly accounting for the finite-range nature of the ion-atom interaction. We show that the interaction range introduces a characteristic momentum scale at which many-body dressing and dissipation are maximized, leading to a non-monotonic behavior of the damping rate and quasiparticle energy. In the high-momentum regime, we uncover a scaling law $Γ_p \sim 1/p$, signaling the suppression of many-body dressing and the recovery of quasi-free impurity dynamics, in stark contrast to the divergent behavior predicted by contact-interaction perturbative treatments.
summary: This paper theoretically examines how charged impurities (polarons) behave in a Bose-Einstein Condensate at finite momentum. By using a diagrammatic approach, the authors show that incorporating the finite range of the ion-atom interaction fundamentally changes the predicted damping rate compared to simple contact models. The key finding is the emergence of a characteristic momentum scale and a physically realistic high-momentum decay law ($\Gamma_p \sim 1/p$).
main_problem: The study investigates the finite-momentum behavior of charged Bose polarons, moving beyond static properties to understand transport and quasiparticle stability.
main_result: The finite-range ion-atom interaction leads to a non-monotonic damping rate peaking at a characteristic momentum scale, and crucially, predicts a high-momentum scaling law $\Gamma_p \sim 1/p$, unlike contact interaction predictions.
method: The analysis employs a diagrammatic approach using imaginary-time Green's functions and perturbative calculations up to second order in the ion-boson coupling.
model_or_system: The system models a charged impurity (ion) coupled to a Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) of bosons. The Hamiltonian includes terms for kinetic energy, boson-boson interaction, and the ion-boson coupling potential.
key_observables: Quasiparticle energy ($\omega_p$), damping rate ($\Gamma_p$), and quasiparticle residue ($Z_p$).
important_parameters: Momentum ($p$), interaction range scale ($b$), and the boson-boson scattering length ($a_{BB}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to weak coupling and perturbative treatment up to second order; it assumes the BEC is well-described by Bogoliubov theory.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the non-monotonic dependence of $\Gamma_p$ and $\Delta E$ on momentum, the scaling $\Gamma_p \propto 1/p$ at high momentum, and the dependence of the residue $Z$ on the boson-boson scattering length $a_{BB}$.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory by calculating the self-energy ($\Sigma_2$) via Feynman diagrams, determining quasiparticle properties from the Green's function poles, and comparing the results obtained using a realistic finite-range potential versus a simple contact interaction.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it provides detailed, momentum-resolved predictions for impurity dynamics in quantum gases, offering a crucial theoretical benchmark for interpreting future momentum-resolved transport measurements in ultracold atomic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic phase diagram of the finite-momentum charged Bose polaron for the ion-atom interaction. The ver- tical dashed line marks the Landau threshold. Above this threshold, quasiparticle dressing is strongest around the char- acteristic scale p ∼1/b. For larger momenta or larger effec- tive ranges, the impurity enters a weakly dressed, quasi-free regime where the damping is suppressed. The red shadow in- dicates the non-perturbative regime associated with two-body bound-state formation. The blue curve marks p = 1/b.

FIG. 2. Scattering length akn of the ion–atom potential as a function of the dimensionless parameter bkn. For sufficiently large b, the system lies in the perturbative regime. As b de- creases, akn exhibits a divergence signaling the onset of a bound state in the two-body problem. The shaded region indicates the regime where bound states emerge and the per- turbative description breaks down.

FIG. 3. Second-order Feynman diagrams contributing to the self-energy of the charged polaron. Solid blue lines denote the ion propagator, solid green lines represent bosonic prop- agators, wavy lines indicate the ion-boson interaction, and dashed lines correspond to condensate lines. Panels (a)–(d) account for different processes including normal and anoma- lous BEC propagators.

FIG. 4. Damping rate Γp/En as a function of momentum p/kn for (top) contact and (bottom) ion-atom interactions, shown for equivalent impurity-boson scattering lengths. Solid (dashed) lines correspond to an interacting (ideal) Bose gas. The shaded regions indicate the dissipationless, dissipative, and perturbative breakdown regime (red shadow) or quasi- free regime (green shadow)

FIG. 5. Momentum dependence of the scaled damping rate p Γp/En for different interaction strengths akn. This reveals that all curves approach a constant asymptotic value, demon- strating the asymptotic high-momentum scaling Γp ∝1
